story:
I love love love chocolate. It makes me giggle and grin and feel like everything is the best ever. Last Friday, at Maya’s birthday party, chocolate turned from a once-in-a-while treat into my everything. Maya’s parents rented a huge yellow bus and took all of us to a big farm. There was a giant pink princess piñata stuffed with mixed sweets, mountains of water balloons, and space to run forever. I hid among the older kids (I’m only three!) and ate, ate, ate the candy I scooped. The sugar made me so happy and so zippy that I decided life should always feel like this. I love love love chocolate!

At first, I took tiny tastes. A single chocolate kiss from the grocery store checkout—so small no one would miss it, I thought. Then more. Kids at the park shared. I found pieces at home. My little hands were quick, my smile was cute, and my tummy wanted sugar all the time. But whispers started: “missing candies.” Dad stared at the kitchen like a detective. “Fernanda, where did the cookies go? And my special dark chocolate?” The teacher of my preschool called, too. Dad waited for me at the door one hot, sleepy afternoon. He knelt down, held my sticky hands, and said in his serious-soft voice that kids were sad because I kept taking their candy, and parents noticed things missing after we visited. My stomach flip-flopped.

“But it’s chocolate,” I whispered. “It makes me happy.”

“Chocolate is nice,” Dad said. “But we don’t take what isn’t ours. And too much candy hurts—teeth, tummy, even your thinking. We need real food to grow strong. Not just sugar, sugar, sugar.”

I didn’t want to hear it. I stomped away, slammed my door—BAM—and pulled out my secret stash from under my pillow. I tore into a big bar, then another, and another, until my belly ached and my heart went thump-thump-thump too fast. The room wobbled. The flowers on my blanket melted into chocolate swirls. The bumpy wood floor turned into a smooth, dark slab like a giant bar. The air smelled thick and sweet, like a factory.

Everything became Chocolate Land. My walls were waves of dark chocolate with white chocolate streams. My little chair was caramel chocolate. My books were stacks of milk, dark, and white bars with sprinkles. I poked the wall; my finger sank in—mmm, chocolate! I giggled and slid onto a giant chocolate coin rug, happy again.

Then a shadow: Dad—made of dark chocolate, with white chocolate-chip eyes. Mom, too, beautiful milk-and-white chocolate curls. Their faces looked worried but kind. “What have you done?” Dad rumbled, melty and deep. “This is your Chocolate Dream,” he said gently. “But not everything is chocolate. Too much of anything can make the world small.” Mom added, “Treats are special because they’re not everywhere. We savor them.”

I looked around. It was all brown. No mangoes. No colors. No bumpy, squishy, crunchy, fresh. The joy felt sticky. “I don’t want everything to be chocolate,” I thought.

The walls rippled. Blue peeked through. The floor turned back to wood. The smell thinned. My chocolate parents softened and faded. “Time to wake up,” Dad said.

I opened my eyes in my real room, wrappers everywhere. My hands were just hands. I still loved chocolate—love love love it—but I understood something new: the world needs variety, and taking what isn’t mine is wrong. I cleaned up the wrappers, went to the kitchen, and hugged Dad. “I’m sorry,” I said. “I want lots of foods, not only chocolate.”
